metadata.title: Why Every Qubit Lives on a Globe
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = Bloch sphere surface / pure states; CRIMSON = global phase / unobservable
metadata.note: Exclusions: no expectation-value derivation of the Bloch vector, no gate operations, no theta/2 double-cover subtlety (spinor 720 is Card 15).

beats.narration_text: A quantum bit can be spin-up, spin-down, or any superposition. How do you picture all the possible superpositions at once? Every one of them is a point on the surface of a sphere.

card.copy: Why Every Qubit Lives on a Globe

beats.narration_text: A qubit state has two complex amplitudes. That seems like four real parameters. But normalization fixes one, and a global phase is unobservable. That removes two more. Two real numbers remain — exactly the two angles needed to point to a spot on a sphere.
Four complex parameters. Normalization and global phase each remove one. Two free parameters. Why does that give a sphere, and what are the poles?

card.copy:
A qubit has 4 real parameters.
Normalization removes 1. Global phase removes 1.
2 remain. Why a sphere?

beats.narration_text: The two remaining free parameters are the polar angle theta and the azimuthal angle phi. They parametrize the sphere exactly. The state is cosine of theta-over-two times spin-up, plus e-to-the-i-phi times sine of theta-over-two times spin-down.

The north pole is theta equals zero: pure spin-up. The south pole is theta equals pi: pure spin-down. The entire equator is where theta equals pi over two — equal superpositions, differing only in relative phase.

The azimuthal angle phi is the relative phase between spin-up and spin-down. A different phi at the same theta means a different superposition — with different measurement outcomes for x or y spin. Relative phase is physically real and distinguishable.

What is the global phase? Multiplying the entire state by e-to-the-i-gamma shifts both amplitudes equally. No measurement of any observable can detect it. The Bloch sphere automatically collapses it: two states differing only in global phase map to the same point.

The Bloch vector provides a direct sanity check. Compute the three expectation values — sigma-x, sigma-y, sigma-z. They form a vector whose length must equal exactly one for any pure state. If you compute a state and its Bloch vector has length less than one, you made an error.

Every physical qubit — an electron spin, a photon polarization, a superconducting circuit — has its state represented by a point on this sphere. Quantum gates move the point across the sphere's surface. The north and south poles are the computational basis states zero and one.

A qubit has four real parameters. Remove global phase and normalization — two remain: theta and phi. Two angles, one sphere. Poles are definite spin. Equator is equal superposition. Relative phase is real and distinguishable.

card.copy:
4 params minus normalization minus global phase = 2.
Theta and phi place you on a sphere.
Poles: |0> and |1>.
Equator: equal superpositions.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Foundations: The Quantum World series.

props.seriesTitle: Quantum Mechanics: Foundations: The Quantum World
